title: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier: extending time, organogenesis-in-a-dish, and in-vitro gestation

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier: extending time, organogenesis-in-a-dish, and in-vitro gestation
We're approaching the end.
Let me end with the frontier — where the field is going, and what some of the further-out possibilities look like.
Frontier one: extending the time window.
The most immediate frontier is pushing synthetic embryos further along the developmental timeline.
In mouse, the current ceiling is around day 8.5 with Hanna's system.
The next step is pushing further — toward day 10, day 12, day 14, and beyond.

Each additional day of in vitro development is a major technical challenge, requiring better culture systems, better gas exchange, better waste removal, better nutrient delivery, better vascular support.
Multiple groups are working on this.
Progress is incremental but real.
In humans, the equivalent push is from the current limit — early post-implantation, around day 14 — toward later stages.
The technical challenges are even greater than for mouse, and the ethical scrutiny is more intense.
But the same trajectory applies.
Each year, the time ceiling moves slightly.
Where we'll be in five years is uncertain but likely meaningfully further than where we are now.
Frontier two: organogenesis-in-a-dish.
As synthetic embryos push past the gastrulation stage, they enter organogenesis — the period when individual organs first form.

A synthetic embryo at the right developmental stage has the makings of a heart, a brain, a kidney, a liver — all developing in their proper embryonic positions, with their proper signaling environments.
This is potentially transformative for studying organogenesis, for modeling congenital disorders, and for understanding how the body plan unfolds.
There's a more speculative version of this idea: using synthetic embryos as developmental scaffolds for organ generation.

The thought is that if you can get a synthetic embryo to develop a particular organ in its proper environment, you might be able to isolate that organ — or guide its development toward producing transplant-quality tissue.
This is, currently, speculative.
The synthetic embryos don't develop far enough to make any organs transplant-ready.
But the underlying logic is interesting and may, over a longer timescale, become a real application.
Frontier three: vascularization.

One of the biggest limitations of all stem cell-derived models — synthetic embryos, organoids, you name it — is that they don't have functional blood vessels supplying nutrients to the interior of the structure.
As the structure grows beyond a millimeter or so, the interior starves and dies.
Solving the vascularization problem would allow much larger, more functional models.
Multiple approaches are being pursued — co-culture with vascular cells, engineered vascular networks, microfluidic perfusion systems, and so on.
Progress is happening.
Vascularized synthetic embryos, with internal circulation, would be a major advance.
Frontier four: combining with maternal-tissue models.
Real embryos develop in interaction with maternal tissue — the uterus, the placenta, the maternal blood supply.
Synthetic embryos in dishes don't have any of this.
Several groups are working on combining synthetic embryos with endometrial organoids — stem-cell-derived models of the uterine lining — to recreate the embryo-maternal interaction in vitro.
Early work shows that this can be done at the implantation stage.
More advanced versions could allow modeling of placentation, of pregnancy-related disorders, of maternal-fetal cross-talk.
Frontier five: artificial wombs.
This is the most speculative possibility, and it deserves a careful framing.
Right now, in 2025, no one is gestating mammals from synthetic embryos in artificial wombs.
The technology doesn't exist.
But there are research efforts in two adjacent areas that, taken together, point in this direction.
One is the kind of work Hanna's lab is doing — pushing synthetic embryos further developmentally in ex utero culture.

The other is artificial placenta work for premature infants — the "biobag" experiments by the Children's Hospital of Philadelphia group and others, which have kept extremely premature lambs and other animals alive ex utero for weeks, supporting late-fetal development outside a real uterus.
Neither of these is the same as gestating an organism from a synthetic embryo.
But you can see, in principle, how progress on both fronts could eventually meet in the middle.
If synthetic embryos can be pushed further toward viability, and artificial placentas can be pushed further toward earlier-stage support, the two could, eventually, converge.
This is decades away, at minimum.
It may never happen at all — there are many technical reasons it might not work.
But it's a possibility that's been discussed seriously enough that the ISSCR guidelines and other ethical frameworks have started to address it.

I want to be careful here, because this kind of speculation is the part of the field that most readily becomes science fiction, and the gap between current capability and full in vitro gestation is enormous.
We shouldn't oversell the proximity.

But we also shouldn't dismiss the trajectory, because the underlying technologies are improving, and the question of what we'd do if it became feasible is worth thinking about now rather than after the fact.
If, at some future date, it became technically possible to gestate a mammal from a synthetic embryo to viability in a fully artificial environment, the ethical implications would be profound.

It would, depending on your view, either be a remarkable medical advance — potentially saving lives, transforming reproduction, allowing pregnancies that couldn't otherwise happen — or a deeply concerning extension of human technological intervention into the most fundamental biological process.
Probably both.
The international community would, presumably, want to have agreed on a framework before any such thing was attempted in humans.
Getting that framework right requires the conversation to start now, not later.
Let me bring this all together.
Synthetic embryos started, in 2018, with mouse blastoids — proof that stem cells could be coaxed to self-organize into structures resembling early embryos.
In 2021, human blastoids appeared.
In 2022 and 2023, mouse synthetic embryos reached day 8.5 with hearts and brains.
In 2023, human ETX models pushed through early post-implantation and into the start of gastrulation.
The frontier continues to move.
These structures are scientifically important.
They give us, for the first time, real experimental access to the period of human development that has been hidden from us for as long as we've been trying to understand ourselves.
The first two to four weeks of human life — the period when most miscarriages happen, most birth defects originate, most failed IVF attempts fail — has been a black box.
Synthetic embryos are opening it.
These structures are also ethically important.
They blur the definition of embryo.
They challenge the frameworks that bioethics has used for decades.
They raise the question of what we owe to embryo-like structures that aren't, in the traditional sense, embryos.
The 14-day rule has been modified.
The ISSCR has updated its guidelines.
National regulations are catching up.
The conversation is ongoing.
I don't think there are easy answers here.
The science is going to keep advancing — the trajectory is clear, the techniques are spreading, the funding is in place.
The question isn't whether the field will continue but how it will continue, with what oversight, and toward what applications.
If you walk away from this lecture remembering one thing, let it be this.
You take stem cells, put them together with the right signals, and they self-organize into structures that look and behave like embryos.
The cells contain the program for being an embryo.
We've found, with great care and effort, how to evoke that program in a dish, without an egg, without a sperm, without a uterus.

That fact — once you understand it — is going to keep showing up in the news, in the science journals, in the bioethics literature, in the legal and political conversations about what humans should and shouldn't do with the technology we have.
You'll encounter this in many forms over the coming years.

I hope that, having walked through it with me today, you'll be able to recognize what's going on, distinguish careful science from sensational reporting, and think clearly about the questions the field is raising.
The story of mammalian development used to start with a fertilized egg.
It still does, in nature.
But in our labs, increasingly, it can start with a skin cell.
And what that means for biology, for medicine, and for our concepts of embryo, pregnancy, and reproduction — that's the question of the coming decade.
That's all I have.
Thank you for your attention, and good luck with the rest of your studies.
